facts: The paper proposes a three-stage LLM-based pipeline for multi-source meeting summarization: (1) gap identification, (2) information inferring via RAG, and (3) summarization of the enriched transcript.
The multi-source approach improves summary relevance by approximately 9% compared to single-source summarization.
The personalization protocol improves informativeness by approximately 10% over a simple personalized baseline.
The authors introduce MS-AMI, a dataset of 125 multi-source meeting samples based on the AMI corpus, including supplementary materials such as whiteboard drawings, slides, and notes.
MS-AMI was created by removing 12 samples from the original 137 AMI meetings due to processing errors.
GPT-4o was used for OCR and image description, and Aspose was used for document text extraction when building MS-AMI.
The primary backbone model used in experiments is GPT-4 Turbo (gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09).
The pipeline limits gap identification to the top five most relevant context gaps to balance information coverage and computational effort.
OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small model and cosine similarity are used for RAG-based retrieval in the pipeline.
Summaries are limited to 250 tokens to align with comparable works using the AMI corpus.
The best-performing general pipeline setup (G-infer) achieves an informativeness score of 4.49 and a factuality score of 4.78 on a 5-point Likert scale.
G-infer outperforms the single-source baseline (G-none) by 0.42 points in overall quality and 0.45 points in factuality.
The G-all setup (all sources appended to transcript) achieves the highest relevance score of 4.11 but suffers from repetition errors due to redundant supplementary files.
G-top, which uses only the top 5 RAG-retrieved sources, performs worst among multi-source setups, likely due to insufficient information in some documents.
The full personalized pipeline (P-infer+per) achieves the highest personalized informativeness (INF-P) score of 4.51 and overall personalized score (OVR-P) of 4.79.
Persona-based personalization improves relevance by up to 0.44 compared to a baseline using only the target audience's role information.
The personalization protocol extracts participant personality traits (Big Five), viewpoints, interests, and knowledge levels from the transcript to generate personas.
Scores vary across participant roles, with 'Project Manager' often yielding higher scores, and deviations of up to 0.40 observed across roles.
The paper evaluates four model families for practical deployment: GPT-4 Turbo, Phi-3 mini 3.8b 128k, Gemini Flash 1.5, and Llama 3 8b.
GPT-4 costs approximately $0.25 per sample for general summarization and $0.37 for personalized summarization, taking about 110 and 152 seconds respectively.
Phi-3 mini costs approximately $0.007 per sample and is the fastest model at approximately 32 seconds for general and 44 seconds for personalized summarization.
Llama 3 is the cheapest model at approximately $0.001 per sample but requires a sequential chunking approach due to its 8k token limit.
Phi-3 mini often outperforms the larger Llama 3, likely due to Llama 3's limitations with hierarchical summarization.
Gemini Flash produces high-level but shallow summaries, while Phi-3 closely matches GPT-4 quality with occasional detail gaps.
LLM-based evaluation metrics (AUTOCALIBRATE and FACTSCORE-inspired GPT-4 scoring) achieve human-label matching accuracies of REL: 87.3%, INF: 92.4%, FAC: 85.7%, and OVR: 93.6%.
Inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's alpha) ranges from 0.813 to 0.856 across the four main evaluation metrics.
GPT-4 reliably extracts personas with acceptance rates of 83% (UE), 94% (PM), 87% (ID), and 92% (M) as assessed by human annotators.
The pipeline uses zero-shot LLM capabilities without requiring domain-specific training or few-shot examples.
The paper uses chain-of-thought reasoning in the gap identification stage to identify and prioritize context-deficient passages.
Inferred answers from supplementary materials are inserted into the original transcript as comments enclosed in square brackets.
ROUGE and BERTScore are not reported as main metrics because they yield nearly identical scores across pipeline variants.
The dataset and code are publicly available via GitHub at https://github.com/FKIRSTE/emnlp2024-personalized-meeting-sum.
The MS-AMI dataset has an average of 558.4 turns, 4.0 speakers, and 6567.9 words per meeting, with an average gold summary length of 185.5 words.
The personalization pipeline's P-per setup outperforms P-infer in OVR-P score, indicating the positive influence of persona consideration when evaluating personalization.

The paper notes that incorporating supplementary sources improves summary quality by at least 0.31 over the single-source baseline, with an additional 0.11 improvement when distributing multi-source challenges across multiple LLM instances.
